result:
The first time I realized that time might not flow strictly forward was on an overcast Tuesday when a leaf fell upward from the sidewalk and tucked itself back onto a branch. I blinked, blamed the angle of the light, and kept walking to the stationery store where I intended to buy a pen that felt heavy enough to make my grocery lists feel momentous. But when I lifted the brass tester off its velvet pad, the ink inside behaved like liquid night, swirling with small, silver parentheses I eventually recognized as yesterday’s doubts. Store music looped through the same four measures; even the cashier’s smirk reset every twenty seconds, so I left without paying. Theft seemed irrelevant under the circumstances.  

Outside, entire blocks flickered between summer and late fall like a projector skipping frames. Someone rode a bicycle backward in perfect reverse, exchanging coins for a newspaper that re-folded itself. Nothing else seemed amiss to the rest of the pedestrians, who navigated the stutter with practiced nonchalance. I stood at the intersection of 14th and What-Could-Be, watching rain fall up from the drains into pregnant clouds, and felt the unmistakable urge to take notes. Notes implied order, and order implied a future—apparently a negotiable commodity.

Mara, the barista who can always guess what strangers need before they speak, once told me that déjà vu is simply the mind buffering. “Your brain downloads the next chunk of your life ahead of schedule,” she explained, thumbing cinnamon into a latte destined for an architect whose blueprints kept evaporating at dawn. I liked that version; it suggested a courteous universe that warns us when an alley is about to turn into a Sunday we’ve lived already. Still, nobody explains what to do when the glitch lasts longer than a shiver—when Tuesday extends in both directions until it becomes a soft, wooly corridor, humming the way caves hum when you stand inside them and pretend the stone is breathing.

I looped back toward the river, past the bakery that smells like nostalgia in pastry form. The chalkboard sign I’d chalked myself last week had reverted to blank slate, awaiting the joke I was now formulating in hindsight: “Fresh bread, served on the inexorable march toward entropy.” But the task felt fraudulent; entropy itself looked busy elsewhere, distracted by how neatly the wind brushed leaves into ascending spirals. Inside the shop, dough rose downward like candle wax cooling in reverse time-lapse. I bought a brioche anyway; sweetness is a stubborn constant, even when causation has the hiccups.

By the river, benches existed in partial autumn, occupied by the same old men playing chess with missing kings and queens that periodically reincarnated to reenact historic blunders. I sat near the willow whose reflection rippled into a version of tomorrow: roots snaking down from clouds, thirsty for sky. The brioche tasted like vanilla seashells; with each bite, crumbs rearranged themselves into whole pastry on my lap as though I hadn’t eaten at all. A nearby street musician bowed a violin whose strings rewound into gut and horsehair. The melody gathered itself into a seed, tucked itself into his pocket, and the case lay empty except for yesterday’s coins, slick with lake-fog.

I tried to recall how linearity had once felt-felt, the way each footstep abandoned the next, trusting the sidewalk’s promise of continuation. That trust now felt quaint—like expecting a book to read itself aloud rather than shimmer ambiguously between pages. From my jacket I pulled the smooth brass pen, heavy enough to dent minutes, and opened the memo pad to jot an anchor: “Remember the smell of asphalt after the rain that hasn’t arrived yet.” The ink floated half an inch above the paper, snagging on invisible fibrils of possibility, then settled into letters my hand had not written: “We apologize for the inconvenience, please enjoy this intermission.”

I laughed; the sound spread like rings in pond water, ran headlong into a passing jogger who paused midstride to applaud politely before jogging backward into yesterday’s sunrise. An elderly woman beside me smiled as though we shared a cosmic inside joke, which perhaps we did. “Weather’s peculiar today,” she said, the same phrase I’d hear her say every loop for seventeen iterations. On the eighteenth, I answered differently: “Yes, but rain always forgets its umbrella.” She blinked, startled; her ledger of conversations had not budgeted for improvisation. The glitch shivered, skipped, and suddenly the jogger bolted forward again, chasing sunlight in orthodox fashion. The old woman resumed her knitting a scarf that unraveled into balls still grazing sheep in distant fields.

A gull dove to steal the brioche’s phantom twin; the instant its beak touched pastry, the bird unfurled into a memory I hadn’t realized belonged to me—age twelve, feeding gulls on a coast now eroded by surges we once called myth. Water rushed my shoes though the riverbank remained high and dry. Salt crusted my wrists like pearl dust. Somewhere between pastiches, I understood the rewinding day was not malfunction but instruction: it kept repeating until I admitted I’d left something unfinished in that childhood memory, a hug never delivered to my younger self who feared endings. I closed my eyes and stepped sideways into the scene; the gull circled me, a soft tangle of wings. I hugged the skinny, sunburned child who tasted of salt and quiet rebellion. When I let go, the bird reverted to feathers in the wind, and the river flowed one direction again—if only slightly steadier.

Back on the actual bench, the navy clouds broke apart like theater curtains. The musician pulled the violin from his pocket—now full-size—and began a tune I’d danced to at my first wedding, the one that dissolved not in divorce but in mislaid calendars. This time the notes walked forward, carrying passengers willing to board: an accountant whistling off-key, a teenager translating treble clef into Morse code skirted around missing beats with hopeful feet. I joined the sidewalk parade simply by walking. The pen clinked against my keys, already warm, as if rehearsing sentences for an entire lifetime of Tuesdays.

At the stationery store, the grating fluorescents dimmed to candle-glow. The owner, a woman I’d never seen before even though I’d been there moments earlier, offered tea scented with ginger and dénouement. We spoke about the migration paths of commas, how they flew south when sentences became overwrought. She placed in my hand a notebook bound in starling-sky leather; when I asked the price, she shrugged and quoted the cost in anticipatory laughter—three undelivered jokes yet to occur. I signed my name on the ledger. The ink bled forward in time, drying yesterday’s argument with a friend who would forgive me tomorrow; it felt expedient.

Night, when it finally resolved from precipitation’s indecision, folded the city accordion-style. Apartment balconies overlapped centuries: someone watered petunias in 1978, another toasted with carbonated hope in 2049. My building presented its façade from five simultaneous renovations, all competing for dwelling rights like timelines auditioning for residency. I climbed stairs that rebuilt themselves behind me in quieter wood. Inside my apartment, every lightbulb glowed with a different phase of dusk. I set the new notebook on the windowsill; it opened to a page already half-filled with my handwriting describing exactly this moment. A margin note read: P.S. Buy soap tomorrow. I laughed—there it was, the deep cut of ordinary ambition stitching the fantastic day together.

And yet, before I turned off the lamp, I noticed one line of fresh ink leading into the desk drawer. I slid it open. Inside lay yesterday’s brioche, crumbs coalescing into intact golden swirl. I took a deliberate bite that finally removed it from plate, pocket, continuity. It tasted of rainfall and vanilla, of ladders we climb into clouds and the soft decisions we call seconds. Outside, Tuesday had ended at last, its tail costatic dissolving into Wednesday’s crisp audition. I closed the window against the breeze that whispered — audibly, in my grandmother’s voice — “Write the next page forward, darling. The rewrite is only for pruning, not for hiding.”

So I dipped the brass pen, whose weight now felt like a small bell still chiming inside mountains, and began. The nib left trails so bright the paper ignited softly, a hearth in miniature. I described wooden floors that chose to be boats, city pigeons plotting a parliament, children whose jump ropes measured orbit instead of heartbeats. I wrote until font merged with the sound of rain returning—this time downward, proper, almost embarrassed by its earlier mischief. When the page brimmed, I flipped it. Blank space greeted me with the tender vacuum of unopened mornings, ready for whatever direction time deemed worth traveling next.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
